speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us the-- the story about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and I worked at this place. It was called Ivan K, and it was on Number 112, John R. Street in Detroit. Now, I worked there from October-- yes, October. But in the meantime, Henry Abrams, a man who got me over. His daughter was a secretary to this man you see in the picture up there, Herman Schwartz, who was the maitre d' at the Sheraton Cadillac Hotel. And she told him about me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he booked me for a little party, you know? And it was a party for colored people who were very, very influential people in the City of Detroit. It was a trio to play whilst at dinner. But in the meantime, I was working with this Mickey [? Wolf ?] to see what the format was, how they did things. Because they don't do things the same way amongst Jewish people in England as they do here. You see? And I went and did this party. And the wife had been-- she had been a student at the Sorbonne in France. You know what the Sorbonne University is? And I spoke to her in French, and played French songs. And this man was so happy he gave me a $50 tip on top of what I was paid. Of course, if a person recommends you, you'd better be good, or else he's not going to recommend you any more. And I did well, and started to find out what was what and what was when. And I was starting to get bookings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this fellow Mickey [? Wolf, ?] who I was talking about, that I was working together with him, and we did quite a bit of stuff in Yiddish and everything-- Yiddish songs and everything. And we did quite a bit of stuff, until one day he said to me, Zan, he says, I don't like you working those jobs you're working. I said, hey, Mickey, I didn't come here to be a sideman. I'm a band leader. But in the work that I'm getting at the hotel you don't get because it's Gentile work. You only get the Jewish work. But I said, I'll tell you what I'll do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I said to him, if you want to make a partnership, I'll make a partnership. He looked at me and said, you have been here two months, and I've been here 20 years, and you want to make a partnership? Forget it. So I said, forget it. But later, he came on, and wanted to book me for a party. He booked me as a band leader, giving me a little extra. He booked me for a wedding. Now, my niece in New York-- this was in March of 1961. My niece in New York was getting married. That is the one that lost her father in Sachsenhausen. Now this person had-- that he-- Mickey [? Wolf ?] had booked me for to do the wedding was on the same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: She called me. She said, Uncle Zanny, I want you to stand up with me on the chuppah, because-- you know, with mother. Can I refuse? And I called Mickey, and I told him, Mickey, and I told him the whole story. He says, OK. Half hour later, this woman who I was supposed to do this wedding for calls me and says, how can you do this to me? I've got a contract from you. And-- and you-- I said, I signed no contract with you. She says, I gave you $100 deposit. I said, you didn't give me $100. You gave it to Mickey Wolf.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then this Mickey Wolf calls me after I told this woman it wasn't me. So she went for him. She calls me, this woman. Mickey called me and says, I'm going to run you out of town on a rail. I says, try it, and I says, and I'll put you in a place where the birds won't even drop on you. So anyhow, we broke up. He was flying down to Florida July the 21st. Coming back in a rainstorm, he crashed in his plane, and he and his wife was killed. And all the work started to come to me-- of the Jewish work started to come to me. And it was from then onwards that I made a success. And also, being able to converse with people in their own language-- that Italian, French-- because Detroit is a very polyglot city. As a matter of fact, people, when I used to speak with them in Yiddish, people used to say to me, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Because they had a nickname--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what concentration camp were you-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] were you from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I wasn't in a concentration camp. I was in the English [INAUDIBLE]. And then they, you know, they were surprised, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you continued doing this--

speaker: interviewee
text: I continued doing this--

speaker: interviewer
text: --this Jewish work.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time, very, very busy. Right in front of you there, if you look, please, if you look, you'll see a whole lot of books. This is my life. Every job that I ever did is recorded in those books, and what I got for it, and who worked for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did this until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until Saturday was the last job, August the 13th, 1976.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened on August 13, 1976?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the last job I did, because I-- six months before that-- it is funny how advertising has a big kick. I used to advertise in every week. But once I took my advertisement out-- because I had made up my mind I was leaving, so why advertise? Because people were booking me two years ahead. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So the phone wasn't ringing anyhow, and it didn't matter to me. On the first of September, 1976, I came here and stayed with a friend of mine from England on Fountain. Here being to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Los Angeles, and it wasn't my kind of place to live. And also, being a Shriner and a Mason, and having a letter of recommendation from where I was living, I came here. And I moved in here December the 15th, 1976.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here being Park La Brea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here-- and here I am, nearly 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been doing any work in those 20 years? What have you been doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were funny. There was one that I did a Jewish, a Yiddish wedding. I did her wedding twice in-- when she was in Detroit. This girl was not a very good-looking girl, but she had a very good-looking account. She was left six million by her grandfather. And she got married again here at the restaurant on Yale and Wilshire. There's a restaurant there. And her father, Ernie, asked me to do the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but I had my violin. But I said, OK, I'll do it, and, you know, just play the ceremony and things like that. And I said to him, Ernie-- her name was [? Swartz ?]-- I said Ernie, the next time she gets married, I'm not going to charge you. It's going to be for nothing. [INAUDIBLE] And that's-- oh, and also, I was called upon at one time. I got a call. They were honoring that guy who-- that Jewish producer that did What's his name? Norman Lear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Norman Lear-- he was given a party at the Beverly Hills Hotel, and they were using 50 violins. So the union called me-- there wasn't that amount there, and asked me if I wanted to do it. So I was there 15 minutes, and got $50.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your daughter. Where-- where is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- now, my daughter-- in-- in 1955, at the age of 19, she wanted to get married. So she called. She called me in London. She called me in London, said, Dad, I want to get married, but I want to get married in England because there's both sides of the family, mom and yours, and eight people will come. Luckily, at that time I was working at the Dorchester Hotel, and with two big bands that I worked with. And they low-balled me. Even-- and also, I had to put the eight guests that came from America-- I had to put them up in a hotel, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was married in London, and from London, she went straight off to Sydney, Australia. That's where she is today. She's a grandmother. And I'm a great-grandfather, and I hear from her every week. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about her. Her husband's name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband's name is Miller.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His first name is Ivan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their-- and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren, is named Shelley-- there's a picture of her there. The mother of that little girl, that Mariam, who's named after my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shelley-- they have how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three children-- one lives in Melbourne, and the other one lives in-- in Canberra.

speaker: interviewer
text: The children's names.

speaker: interviewee
text: The children's name-- Maurice, Shelley, and Anne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one of them has the children, has your grandchildren-- your great-grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shelley. Shelley-- that's the mother. The picture is right there behind you of her in this building-- in this room. Take a look-- that square thing that's there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the one I showed you the cloning of the genetics, which I showed you. And she's very, very happy. She was here on-- she came through on a visit last year. Because every year, I've been going to Chicago for Pesach at my youngest brother's place. And there's always been about 22 people at the table. Last year, we didn't go because he decided that he was going to spend the winter in Boca Raton every winter. He had bought an-- bought an apartment there, and it's too much for his wife to cook for 20 people, you know? [SPEAKING YIDDISH], you know? [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, on my birthday, September the 11th, I'm going for Rosh Hashanah. I will be in Chicago for the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And that's it. Mr. Gilbert, thank you very, very much for taking the time to tell us your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: We hope that from now on, you have great joy and [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] from your daughter, from your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: I already have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I hope, we hope, it continues. Thank you, again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gilbert, will you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that must have been taken around 1924, because my dad was made Chairman of the Jewish Lodge of the Grand Order Sons of Jacob. And that's my mother and my father dressed in their very best to go to the [SPEAKING HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Yaakov Sheldon, and my mother's name is Miriam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both Gilbert.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gilbert, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell us about this picture, Mr. Gilbert?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this picture was taken right after the war finished, when my two brothers came home on leave. There's mama, Shifra [INAUDIBLE], Chana, Blima, Zelda, [? Suma ?], and Heschel Zvi, Harry, that's Sid, [? Nita ?], Blanche, Sylvia, Rosie, and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: All your brothers and sisters with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all my brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but they were all brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother. And the picture was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Richmond, London, where my mother was living because our original house had been bombed, partly blasted, and they were repairing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The picture was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Richmond, Creswell Road. Number 27 Creswell Road in the garden of the 27 Creswell Road.

speaker: interviewer
text: In late 1945 or 1946?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my lovely wife, Devorah, Doris. Levy was her maiden name, was killed by a V2 rocket on the date that you see there, May the 14th, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gilbert, would you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [INTERPOSING VOICES] this was taken--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was taken when my-- my son-in-law and my daughter, and the family came on leave from Australia. He was working for Exxon. This was in 1972.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture is my daughter, Jaqueline and her husband, Ivan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken in this apartment. This is Shelley, my granddaughter, and I in this apartment in '81 when she came on a visit, going through on her way from Sydney, Australia, here, and to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my wonderful granddaughter, Mariam, who was, that's been taken in Sydney, Australia. Was taken last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your great-granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my great-granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken last year in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was taken on July the 5th, 1965 at the Raleigh House in Detroit. I'm standing there with my string orchestra and the harp. And that is me in the white jacket, Zang Gilbert.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you again, Mr Gilbert for taking the time to give us this interview for the-- for the Shoah Foundation. We deeply appreciate it.
